particle_size_range: Less than 5 mm (operationally defined by collection method: 0.125 mm mesh for stormwater/wastewater/sediment; >0.355 mm for surface water manta trawl)
key_metrics_output: Microparticle and microplastic abundance (particles per liter), particle morphology (fragments, fibers), polymer type (confirmed via Raman or FTIR spectroscopy), particle size distribution, annual discharge estimates (trillions of particles), source characterization (stormwater, wastewater, sediment, fish)
abstract: The San Francisco Bay Microplastics Project is a comprehensive regional study of microplastic pollution in a major estuary. The project developed baseline data on microplastic occurrence in surface water, sediment, and fish, characterized pathways of microplastic entry (urban stormwater and treated wastewater), and investigated transport to adjacent National Marine Sanctuaries. Microparticles were distinguished from confirmed microplastics via spectroscopy. Stormwater from 12 small tributaries contained 1.3–30 microparticles per liter, with fragments (59%) and fibers (39%) predominating. Black fragments potentially from tire wear were identified. Annual stormwater discharge to the Bay was estimated at approximately 7 trillion microplastics per year.
